studies.title: Comparing The Prophylactic Effects of Caffeine versus Aminophylline on the Apnea of Prematurity in Neonates of Kabul City: A randomized clinical trial
studies.health_condition:
Free text:
Prematurity and low birth weight
ICD-10-GM (translation):
P07 - Disorders related to short gestation and low birth weight, not elsewhere classified
Healthy volunteers:
No Entry
studies.brief_summary: Afghanistan has one of the highest neonatal mortality rates in the world and it is estimated at 37/1000 live births. One of the main causes of neonatal mortality in Afghanistan is preterm birth (35%). In such babies apnea or cessation of respiration is a common problem that may cause brain damage in immature babies especially those with a gestation of fewer than 34 weeks. Although Caffeine and Aminiphyllin are the most commonly prescribed drugs for the treatment of apnea in preterm babies, there is disagreement about preventive management of apnea. According to our hypothesis, the risk of apnea  and the requirement for oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation in preterm infants with a gestational age of equal to or less than 34 weeks or a birth weight of less than 1500g will be reduced by caffeine more effectively than Aminophylline. . Therefore we want to compare the prophylactic effects of caffeine versus Aminophylline on the attacks of apnea in preterm neonates with a gestational a
studies.intervention:
Arm 1:
 40 neonates in group A will be managed by Caffeine citrate 20 mg/kg intravenously as the initial dose on the 1st day of life, and then 5 mg/kg intravenously daily as the maintenance dose for the first 10 days of life.

classification.reason: The title suggests an interventional study, and the note indicates a preliminary analysis suggesting it might be an RCT. However, the lack of explicit details about randomization or allocation reduces confidence.

studies.title: Randomised crossover trial of different nasal support systems for the treatment of apnea of prematurity
studies.health_condition:
ICD-10-GM (translation):
P28.4 - Other apnoea of newborn
Healthy volunteers:
No Entry
studies.brief_summary:
About 80% of infants born before 32 weeks of pregnancy develop intermittent episodes involving pauses in breathing (apnea), slow heart rate (bradycardia) and decreases in oxygen levels (hypoxemia; together called apnea of prematurity or AoP). These are related to their yet immature control of breathing. To avoid the frequent decreases in oxygen saturation, ventilatory support is applied. In this context, a continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is delivered to facilitate respiration. Furthermore there is the possibility to apply additional air blasts in periodical intervals expanding the lungs as during a deep inhalation or to make the airflow oscillate.

In order to apply the airflow by nose, two different devices are available which differ in their design and functioning. Therefore, the effect on breathing varies likewise. Both devices are routinely used in many neonatal intensive care units, including ours, for the treatment of AoP. However, it is unclear which device and mode o
studies.intervention:
Arm 1:
Nasal CPAP via variable flow device (Infant Flow; Electro Medical Equipment, Brighton, UK) (PEEP 6 cm H2O)
Arm 2:
Nasal CPAP using a conventional respirator (Stephanie; Stephan, Gackenbach, Germany) with application of respiratory support by Hudson prongs and additional background support, synchronisation via Graseby Respiration Sensor placed on the abdomen (SIMV f=20/min, P insp.: 15 cm H2O, PEEP: 6 cm H2O)
